avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is considerable above 2000 m and moderate below. In a few places, medium to large dry slab avalanches can be triggered by individuals (even from a distance), especially on shady slopes. Danger areas are difficult to recognise away from obvious pillows of wind drifted snow, which is why caution and a defensive choice of route is still advised. In addition, large spontaneous wet snow avalanches and avalanches triggered by people can occur in all aspects. There is a low level of sliding snow activity.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Combined wet and persistent weak layer problem

snowpackStructure.comment: On the sunny slopes, the snowpack briefly carries a (more or less load-bearing) melt-freeze crust, below which it is moist up to high altitudes. There is still soft, dry snow on the shady slopes. The new and wind slab snow of the last week has settled and is often well connected to each other. However, the connection to the angular persistent weak layer below is still poor.

tendency.highlights: The persistent weak layer problem continues to make the situation tense and difficult to calculate in the terrain. Note slight daytime changes on Tuesday.

weatherForecast.comment:
The night will start clear at first, but clouds will increase from the west. There may be light showers in the second half of the night, snow from around 1800 metres. It will remain dry in Lungau.
On Sunday, the sky will often be overcast and short showers may fall, from about 1700 m as snow. In Lungau it will remain dry and some sunshine is possible. The wind will remain weak. Around 0 degrees at 2000 metres.

avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is moderate. A few small, wet avalanches can occur spontaneously or be triggered by a winter sports enthusiast, and medium avalanches are still possible in isolated cases. Individual gliding avalanches are also possible. There is also a slight persistent weak layer problem from around 1600 metres. Avalanches can break in persistent weak layers, in particular with large additional loads.

snowpackStructure.comment: The snowpack is moist to wet and well settled up to high altitudes. Where there was an old snowpack before the snowfall, it still contains weak layers of large faceted crystals, especially near the ground.

tendency.highlights: Gradual decrease in avalanche danger due to melting snow. Note slight daytime changes on Tuesday.

weatherForecast.comment:
Extensive clouds in medium-high layers will pass through during the night.
On Monday, denser clouds and patches of high fog may initially restrict visibility more often. From midday, the sun will often shine alongside soft spring clouds. The wind will be mostly light from east to south. Temperatures at 2000 metres around 0 degrees.

avalancheActivity.comment:
The avalanche risk is moderate. In a few places, spontaneous wet snow avalanches as well as those triggered by people can occur in all aspects below around 2200 m, which usually remain medium-sized. Gliding avalanches also occur in very steep grassy terrain.
In addition, medium to large dry slab avalanches can be triggered by individuals in a few places, especially on shady slopes above the tree line. Most likely at the transition from little to more snow. Danger areas are difficult to recognise, which is why defensive route selection is still advised.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Combined wet and persistent weak layer problem

snowpackStructure.comment: On the sunny slopes, the snowpack is briefly covered with a (more or less stable) melt-freeze crust, below which it is moist to wet up to high altitudes. On the shady slopes, soft, dry snow can still be found from around 1800 metres. The new and wind slab snow of the last week has settled and is often well connected to each other. However, the connection to the angular persistent weak layer below is still poor in places.

tendency.highlights: The persistent weak layer problem is only slowly weakening and remains insidious. There will be slight daytime changes on Tuesday.

weatherForecast.comment:
Extensive clouds in medium-high layers will pass through during the night.
On Monday, denser clouds and patches of high fog may initially cause visibility to be restricted more often. After longer sunny spells, denser cumulus clouds will develop during the day, especially in the Hohe and Niedere Tauern and the Lungauer Nockberge mountains, with isolated short showers possible. Snow will fall up to around 1700 metres. The wind will be mostly light from east to south. Temperatures at 2000 m around 0 degrees, at 3000 m around -6 degrees.

ALBINA.generalHeadline: Weak persistent weak layer and hardly any cold reserve.
